main_summary.summary: Jamila Jones, interviewed in Atlanta on April 27, 2011 by Joe Mosnier for the Smithsonian-LoC Civil Rights History Project, recounts growing up in Montgomery, Alabama, where she rode the Montgomery bus line from age six and joined the NAACP Youth Council under Mrs. Rosa Parks's mentorship alongside Mrs. Williams, who taught her at age twelve to walk adults through the 27-page voter-registration questionnaire. Jones describes the youth-led dimension of the Montgomery Bus Boycott, walking the 381 days, and forming the Montgomery Gospel Trio with Gladys Carter and Minnie Hendricks McCans as elementary-school singers performing at fundraising teas. She recounts traveling at age fourteen to the Highlander Folk School in Monteagle, Tennessee, where police raided a gathering, cut the lights, and where she introduced the "We Are Not Afraid" verse to "We Shall Overcome." She details stealing a car with Antoinette Carson to retrieve Freedom Riders at Reverend Ralph Abernathy's church, the night spent inside as armed whites surrounded the building, and Reverend Solomon S. Seay Sr.'s "doodle bird" parable. Jones traces her shift to Atlanta, joining Bernice Johnson Reagon to co-found the Harambee Singers, and the Pan-Africanist turn in repertoire including "Hands Off Nkrumah" and "We'll Move On Over You."
key_themes: Freedom songs and movement music transmission
NAACP voter-registration work under Rosa Parks
Pan-Africanism and Black Power in movement repertoire
main_summary.historical_significance:
Jones supplies a first-person youth-participant account of the Montgomery Bus Boycott that complicates the standard adult-leadership narrative, documenting how children organized cross-town walks independently and how Rosa Parks and Mrs. Williams trained twelve-year-olds to teach adults the segregationist voter-registration questionnaire. Her testimony anchors the chain of musical transmission from the Montgomery Gospel Trio through Highlander to the Freedom Singers and Harambee Singers, including a contemporaneous account of the night the "We Are Not Afraid" verse entered "We Shall Overcome" during a police raid at Highlander.

The interview also documents the operational mechanics of Freedom Rider reception in Montgomery, including the car-borrowing improvisation with Antoinette Carson and the mass meeting at Reverend Ralph Abernathy's church during which armed whites surrounded the building. Jones's account of Reverend Solomon S. Seay Sr.'s "doodle bird" stand-your-ground parable and her later Pan-Africanist work with Bernice Johnson Reagon's Harambee Singers extends the historical record across the SCLC, SNCC, and post-1965 Black-consciousness phases of the movement.

chapters.title: Montgomery Family and Bus Line
chapters.summary: Jones describes growing up in Montgomery with her mother, grandmother, sister Doris Crenshaw, and younger brother John Alan McQuirter. She and her sister rode the Montgomery bus line during school, experienced bus-driver harassment from age six, and identifies that experience as the wellspring of her connection to the Montgomery Bus Boycott.

chapters.title: Rosa Parks and the NAACP Youth Council
chapters.summary: Jones recounts joining the NAACP Youth Council with her sister as vice president, attending meetings at Rosa Parks's home where Parks read from National Office NAACP papers, taught the youth that voting was central to their lives, and emphasized carrying themselves responsibly. Parks served Kool-Aid after meetings.

chapters.title: The 381 Days Walking
chapters.summary: Jones, eleven when the boycott began, describes the youth's independent organization of cross-town walking routes, her sister's refusal to take rides because they would displace adult walkers, and the joy of the boycott's conclusion. She recalls testing segregation by sitting in the bus front and her child's fascination with touching a white woman's red hair.

chapters.title: Forming the Montgomery Gospel Trio
chapters.summary: Jones traces the origin of the Montgomery Gospel Trio at Alabama State Laboratory High School Friday talent shows, where four girls including Gladys Carter and Minnie Hendricks McCans chose freedom songs as their repertoire. The group became regulars at Montgomery teas raising money for the movement and the churches.

chapters.title: Carnegie Hall and the First Album
chapters.summary: Jones describes Reverend Solomon S. Seay Sr.'s influence at her home church, the trio's travel to New York to perform at a Carnegie Hall fundraiser organized in part by Reverend Sheldon, and the recording of one of the first civil rights movement albums. The naming of the Montgomery Gospel Trio occurred for that album.

chapters.title: Receiving the Freedom Riders
chapters.summary: Jones describes meeting James Bevel and Bernard LaFayette Jr. before the Freedom Rides, then going to Reverend Ralph Abernathy's house where Mrs. Juanita Abernathy reported no available drivers. Jones, Antoinette Carson, and others took Carson's grandfather's car (marking the tire positions with chalk), picked up the beaten Freedom Riders, and returned the car undetected.

chapters.title: The Church Under Siege and the Doodle Bird
chapters.summary: Jones recounts the mass meeting at the Montgomery church the night the Freedom Riders arrived, when armed whites on horses surrounded the building and the congregation stayed inside overnight. She relays Reverend Solomon S. Seay Sr.'s "doodle bird" parable about backing into the sand to protect one's own as the stand-your-ground response.

chapters.title: Freedom Singers and Harambee Singers Origins
chapters.summary: Jones clarifies she was not part of the original Freedom Singers but joined Bernice Johnson Reagon in Atlanta after the Montgomery Gospel Trio. She and Matti Casey Pierce knocked on Reagon's door to continue singing, founding the Harambee Singers from the Pinnett Festival collaboration between school and community.

chapters.title: Highlander and the We Are Not Afraid Verse
chapters.summary: Jones describes the trio's Highlander Folk School visits at age fourteen with Guy Carawan, where exposure to other singers expanded their songwriting. She recounts "Ain't Gonna Ride No Buses No More" and the night police raided Highlander, cut the lights, and her introduction of the "We Are Not Afraid" verse to "We Shall Overcome" as the song that unnerved the shaking policeman.

chapters.title: College Years, Voter Registration and Sit-Ins
chapters.summary: Jones describes Alabama State University college years working with SCLC organizers James Bevel and James Orange on voter registration in Kings Hill and other rural areas, and on sit-ins at five-and-dime stores. She returns to her twelve-year-old voter-registration training under Rosa Parks and Mrs. Williams, learning the 27-page questionnaire and the Preamble to the Constitution to teach adults at their homes.

chapters.title: Harambee Singers and Pan-Africanism
chapters.summary: Jones describes the Black Power and Pan-Africanist turn in her work around 1966, including the Harambee Singers' radical repertoire ("We'll Move On Over You," "Hands Off Nkrumah") and references to the CIA in their songs. The group's repertoire never overlapped with the Montgomery Gospel Trio's; they wore Afros and addressed international as well as domestic struggles.

chapters.title: Community Elders and the John Story
chapters.summary: Jones names community figures who shaped her: Flora Grant at Atlanta Life Insurance Company, Sullivan Rogers among the first Black jockeys, and her storytelling aunt Besidee. She recounts Besidee's "the John" sharecropper-and-chicken-coop story which she later turned into her own song "Pry, boy," reflecting on how older people's impact sustained her movement work into her sixties.
